1:
"Him that is weak in the faith receive ye, but not to doubtful disputations." The witnesses note the reception of the weak believer without disputes. On "him that is weak in the faith," the grammatical voices note the subject. Barnes and Robertson note the "weak" is the believer (chiefly the Jewish convert) whose faith is not strong enough to grasp his full Christian liberty — scrupulous about meats and days, yet a true believer.

On "receive ye," the witnesses note the duty. Barnes and Calvin note the strong are to receive (welcome, admit to full fellowship) the weak brother — not to exclude or despise him for his scruples.

On "not to doubtful disputations," the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note the meaning: not for the purpose of disputing over his doubtful opinions, judging his scruples, or unsettling his conscience by contentious arguments — receive him, but not to wrangle. The verse displays the reception of the weak believer without disputes.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the "weak" is the believer (chiefly the Jewish convert) whose faith cannot yet grasp his full liberty — scrupulous about meats and days, yet truly a believer (Barnes, Robertson) — whom the strong are to receive (welcome to full fellowship), not despising his scruples (Barnes, Calvin); and "not to doubtful disputations" means not for wrangling over his opinions or unsettling his conscience (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays the reception of the weak. The verse marks the subject: the weak in faith. The verse displays the duty: receive ye. The verse displays the manner: not to disputes. The verse displays the charity: welcoming, not wrangling. The reception of the weak displays the charity that bears with a brother's scruples. The verse displays the weakness: of faith. The verse displays the welcome: full fellowship. The verse displays the forbearance: no contention. The reception of the weak believer without disputes — receive him, but not to doubtful disputations — displays the charity that opens the great chapter on the strong and the weak: the strong are to welcome the scrupulous brother into full fellowship, not for the sake of judging or wrangling over his doubtful opinions but in love. The verse displays the law of forbearance that governs all that follows, and illustrates the difference in the matter of meats (v.2).
2:
"For one believeth that he may eat all things: another, who is weak, eateth herbs." The witnesses note the difference between the strong and weak over meats. On "one believeth he may eat all things," the grammatical voices note the strong. Barnes and Robertson note the strong believer is persuaded that, under the gospel, all foods are lawful — the ceremonial distinctions abolished, he may eat anything.

On "another, who is weak, eateth herbs," the witnesses note the scrupulous. Barnes and Calvin note the weak believer, doubtful about meats (perhaps fearing flesh might be unclean or offered to idols), confines himself to vegetables to be safe — eating only herbs.

The eastern Theodoret notes the occasion: the weak (Jewish-minded) abstained from certain meats from conscience; the example concrete and practical, the matter being food. The verse displays the difference between the strong and the weak over meats.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the strong believer is persuaded that under the gospel all foods are lawful, the ceremonial distinctions abolished (Barnes, Robertson); while the weak, doubtful about meats (fearing flesh might be unclean or idol-tainted), confines himself to vegetables to be safe, eating only herbs (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes the weak abstained from conscience. The verse displays the difference over meats. The verse marks the strong: eats all things. The verse displays the weak: eats herbs. The verse displays the cause: scruple of conscience. The verse displays the concreteness: the matter of food. The difference displays the practical occasion of the chapter's counsel. The verse displays the liberty: of the strong. The verse displays the scruple: of the weak. The verse displays the food: the test case. The difference between the strong and the weak over meats — one eats all things, another only herbs — displays the practical occasion of Paul's counsel: the strong, persuaded of their liberty, eat freely, while the weak, doubtful of conscience, restrict themselves to vegetables. The verse displays the concrete matter (food) in which forbearance was needed, and forbids the mutual contempt and judging that arose from it (v.3).
3:
"Let not him that eateth despise him that eateth not; and let not him which eateth not judge him that eateth: for God hath received him." The witnesses note the mutual forbearance: neither despise nor judge. On "let not him that eateth despise," the grammatical voices note the strong's fault. Barnes and Robertson note the strong (who eats all) must not despise (look down on, hold in contempt) the weak as ignorant or superstitious.

On "let not him which eateth not judge," the witnesses note the weak's fault. The weak (who abstains) must not judge (condemn) the strong as lawless or profane — each tempted to a characteristic fault toward the other.

On "for God hath received him," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the ground. Barnes notes that God has received both — the strong and the weak alike accepted by God; so neither should reject whom God has welcomed. The verse displays the mutual forbearance grounded in God's reception of both.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the strong (who eats all) must not despise (hold in contempt) the weak as superstitious, and the weak (who abstains) must not judge (condemn) the strong as lawless (Barnes, Robertson) — each tempted to a characteristic fault — for God has received both, the strong and the weak alike accepted by him (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes neither should reject whom God has welcomed. The verse displays the mutual forbearance. The verse marks the strong's fault: despising. The verse displays the weak's fault: judging. The verse displays the ground: God hath received him. The verse displays the equality: both accepted. The mutual forbearance displays the acceptance of both by God. The verse displays the prohibition: against contempt. The verse displays the prohibition: against condemning. The verse displays the basis: divine reception. The mutual forbearance — neither despise nor judge, for God hath received him — displays the ground of Christian charity in differences of conscience: since God has accepted both the strong and the weak, neither may despise or condemn the other. The verse displays that God's reception of a brother forbids our rejection of him, and presses the point by the relation of a servant to his master (v.4).
4:
"Who art thou that judgest another man's servant? to his own master he standeth or falleth. Yea, he shall be holden up: for God is able to make him stand." The witnesses note the rebuke of judging another's servant. On "who art thou that judgest another man's servant?" the grammatical voices note the rebuke. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: the believer is God's servant, not ours; to judge a fellow servant is to usurp the master's place — it is not our business to condemn whom God owns.

On "to his own master he standeth or falleth," the witnesses note the accountability. The servant answers to his own master (God), who alone is his judge — he stands or falls by God's verdict, not ours.

On "he shall be holden up: for God is able," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the assurance. Barnes notes the gracious promise: the weak servant shall be upheld, for God is able (and willing) to make him stand — God's power preserving his own. The verse displays the rebuke of judging another's servant, with the assurance of God's upholding.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer is God's servant, not ours; to judge a fellow servant usurps the master's place (Barnes, Robertson) — the servant answers to his own master (God), standing or falling by God's verdict — and "he shall be holden up, for God is able to make him stand," the gracious promise that the weak servant shall be upheld by God's power (Barnes, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the rebuke of judging. The verse marks the rebuke: who art thou? The verse displays the accountability: to his own master. The verse displays the assurance: he shall be holden up. The verse displays the power: God is able. The rebuke displays God's sole right to judge his servants. The verse displays the usurpation: judging another's servant. The verse displays the master: God alone. The verse displays the upholding: by God's power. The rebuke of judging another's servant — to his own master he standeth or falleth, and God is able to make him stand — displays God's sole right to judge his own, and his power to preserve them: the weak brother is God's servant, accountable to God alone, and shall be upheld by God's grace. The verse displays both the impropriety of our judging and the security of the believer in God's hand, and turns to the kindred matter of days (v.5).
5:
"One man esteemeth one day above another: another esteemeth every day alike. Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind." The witnesses note the difference over days — and the question of the Sabbath. On "one esteemeth one day above another," the grammatical voices note the scruple. Barnes, Gill, and Calvin note this refers to the Jewish festival days (passover, pentecost, new moons, the ceremonial sabbaths) — the weak (Jewish) believer still regarding them as sacred.

On "another esteemeth every day alike," the great question arises: does this abolish the Christian Sabbath/Lord's Day? Barnes argues strongly that it does NOT — the discussion concerns only the peculiar Jewish ceremonial days, not the Lord's Day, which was observed by all Christians (1 Corinthians 16:2; Acts 20:7). Wesleyan-arminian Clarke insists "alike" is not in the Greek and should not be added, the Sabbath being of lasting obligation as a type of the eternal rest. Ellicott takes the observance of days as a matter of religious expediency.

On "let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind," the Reformed Calvin notes the rule: in these indifferent matters, each must act from full conviction, dependent on the will of God, not in a doubtful or vacillating conscience. The verse displays the difference over days and the call to full persuasion.

The witnesses agree the verse concerns the Jewish festival days (passover, new moons, ceremonial sabbaths) — the weak still regarding them as sacred (Barnes, Gill, Calvin) — and that each must "be fully persuaded in his own mind," acting from full conviction in these indifferent matters (Calvin). But the great question divides: does "every day alike" abolish the Christian Sabbath? Barnes argues strongly it does not (the discussion touching only Jewish ceremonial days, the Lord's Day observed by all Christians); Clarke insists "alike" is no part of the Greek and the Sabbath is of lasting obligation; Ellicott takes days as a matter of expediency. The verse displays the difference over days. The verse marks the scruple: esteeming a day. The verse displays the question: the Sabbath. The verse displays the rule: full persuasion. The verse displays the conviction: in his own mind. The difference over days displays the liberty of conscience in ceremonial observances. The verse displays the festival: Jewish days. The verse displays the debate: the Lord's Day. The verse displays the persuasion: full conviction. The difference over days — one esteems a day above another, another every day alike — displays the liberty of conscience in ceremonial observances, with the great question whether it abolishes the Christian Sabbath: most (Barnes, Clarke) restrict it to the Jewish festival days and uphold the Lord's Day, while each man is to be fully persuaded in his own mind. The verse displays the call to a settled, conscientious conviction in things indifferent, and grounds all such observance in regard to the Lord (v.6).
6:
"He that regardeth the day, regardeth it unto the Lord; and he that regardeth not the day, to the Lord he doth not regard it. He that eateth, eateth to the Lord, for he giveth God thanks; and he that eateth not, to the Lord he eateth not, and giveth God thanks." The witnesses note that both parties act unto the Lord. On "he that regardeth the day, regardeth it unto the Lord," the grammatical voices note the motive. Barnes and Robertson note that the weak who observes the day does so out of regard for the Lord — a conscientious desire to honour God; and the strong who observes it not is likewise acting to the Lord.

On "he that eateth, eateth to the Lord, for he giveth God thanks," the witnesses note the consecration. Both the eater and the abstainer give God thanks — both act from a sincere desire to please and honour God, sanctifying their conduct by thanksgiving.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the charitable judgment: since each, in his differing practice, sincerely seeks to honour God and gives thanks, neither should be condemned; the heart's direction toward the Lord hallows the act. The verse displays that both parties act unto the Lord with thanksgiving.

The witnesses agree: the weak who observes the day does so out of regard for the Lord (a conscientious desire to honour God), and the strong who observes it not is likewise acting to the Lord (Barnes, Robertson); and both the eater and the abstainer give God thanks, acting from a sincere desire to please him, sanctifying their conduct by thanksgiving. Calvin and Theodoret note that since each sincerely seeks to honour God, neither should be condemned. The verse displays that both act unto the Lord. The verse marks the motive: unto the Lord. The verse displays the consecration: thanksgiving. The verse displays the eater: to the Lord. The verse displays the abstainer: to the Lord. Both acting unto the Lord display the charitable judgment of differing practice. The verse displays the regard: for the Lord. The verse displays the thanks: to God. The verse displays the sincerity: of both. That both parties act unto the Lord with thanksgiving displays the charitable judgment that should govern differences of conscience: since each, whether observing or eating freely, sincerely seeks to honour God and gives him thanks, the heart's direction toward the Lord hallows the act and forbids condemnation. The verse displays the common devotion underlying the outward difference, and grounds it in the believer's entire belonging to the Lord (vv.7-9).
7:
"For none of us liveth to himself, and no man dieth to himself." The witnesses note that we live and die not to ourselves but to the Lord. On "none of us liveth to himself," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note that no Christian lives for himself alone — as his own master or for his own ends; his life belongs to and is governed by the Lord.

On "no man dieth to himself," the witnesses note the extension. Even in death the believer is not his own; his death, like his life, is under the Lord's disposal and to the Lord's glory.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the application: since we belong wholly to the Lord in life and death, we are to act not for self but for him — and this includes bearing with our brethren rather than pleasing ourselves. The verse displays that we live and die not to ourselves.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: no Christian lives for himself alone, as his own master or for his own ends — his life belongs to and is governed by the Lord (Barnes, Robertson); and even in death the believer is not his own, his death too under the Lord's disposal and to his glory. Henry and Calvin note that since we belong wholly to the Lord in life and death, we are to act not for self but for him, bearing with our brethren. The verse displays that we live and die not to ourselves. The verse marks the principle: none lives to himself. The verse displays the extension: none dies to himself. The verse displays the belonging: to the Lord. The verse displays the application: not self, but him. That we live and die not to ourselves displays the believer's entire belonging to the Lord. The verse displays the life: not our own. The verse displays the death: not our own. The verse displays the Lord: our master. That we live and die not to ourselves but to the Lord displays the believer's entire belonging to Christ in life and death: no Christian is his own, to live or die for himself, but is governed wholly by the Lord. The verse displays the great truth that undergirds all Christian self-denial and forbearance, and states it directly (v.8).
8:
"For whether we live, we live unto the Lord; and whether we die, we die unto the Lord: whether we live therefore, or die, we are the Lord's." The witnesses note that living or dying, we are the Lord's. On "whether we live, we live unto the Lord," the grammatical voices note the consecration of life. Barnes and Robertson note that the believer's life is wholly devoted to the Lord — lived for his service, his glory, his will.

On "whether we die, we die unto the Lord," the witnesses note the consecration of death. Even dying, the believer is the Lord's — his death an act of submission and devotion, gain in Christ.

On "we are the Lord's," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the comprehensive truth: in life and in death, the believer wholly belongs to Christ, who is Lord of both states — the great ground of comfort and of self-surrender. The verse displays that living or dying, we are the Lord's.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer's life is wholly devoted to the Lord (lived for his service and glory) and his death too is unto the Lord (an act of submission and devotion, gain in Christ, Barnes, Robertson); so that, living or dying, "we are the Lord's." Calvin and Theodoret note the comprehensive truth: in life and death the believer wholly belongs to Christ, Lord of both states — the ground of comfort and self-surrender. The verse displays that we are the Lord's. The verse marks the life: unto the Lord. The verse displays the death: unto the Lord. The verse displays the belonging: we are the Lord's. The verse displays the comfort: Christ Lord of both. That living or dying we are the Lord's displays the believer's total belonging to Christ. The verse displays the consecrated life. The verse displays the consecrated death. The verse displays the ownership: the Lord's. That whether we live or die we are the Lord's displays the believer's total belonging to Christ in every state: his life is lived to the Lord and his death died to the Lord, so that in both he is wholly Christ's own. The verse displays the comprehensive lordship of Christ over the believer, and grounds it in Christ's death and resurrection (v.9).
9:
"For to this end Christ both died, and rose, and revived, that he might be Lord both of the dead and living." The witnesses note Christ Lord of the dead and the living by his death and resurrection. On "to this end Christ both died and rose," the grammatical voices note the purpose. Barnes and Robertson note that Christ's death and resurrection had this as one design: to establish his lordship over both the dead and the living — he died and rose that he might rule over all.

On "Lord both of the dead and living," the witnesses note the universal dominion. Christ, by dying and rising, acquired (or manifested) his sovereign right over all — the departed and the living alike are under his lordship; none escapes his dominion.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the ground: since Christ purchased this lordship by his death and resurrection, the believer belongs to him in life and death, and must live and die to him. The verse displays Christ Lord of the dead and the living.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ's death and resurrection had this design — to establish his lordship over both the dead and the living; he died and rose that he might rule over all (Barnes, Robertson); the departed and the living alike under his dominion, none escaping it. Cyril and Calvin note that since Christ purchased this lordship by his death and resurrection, the believer belongs to him in life and death and must live and die to him. The verse displays Christ Lord of the dead and living. The verse marks the purpose: to this end. The verse displays the means: died and rose. The verse displays the dominion: of dead and living. The verse displays the ground: his lordship purchased. Christ Lord of all displays the foundation of the believer's belonging. The verse displays the death: of Christ. The verse displays the resurrection: of Christ. The verse displays the lordship: over all. Christ Lord of the dead and the living, by his death and resurrection, displays the foundation of the believer's total belonging: Christ died and rose precisely that he might be Lord of both states, so that the believer, in life and in death, is wholly his. The verse displays the purchased and universal dominion of Christ, and from it returns to rebuke the judging of a brother (vv.10-12).
10:
"But why dost thou judge thy brother? or why dost thou set at nought thy brother? for we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ." The witnesses note the rebuke of judging in view of Christ's judgment seat. On "why dost thou judge... set at nought thy brother?" the grammatical voices note the double rebuke. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses both faults: the weak who judges (condemns) and the strong who sets at nought (despises) — both are wrong toward a brother.

On "we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ," the witnesses note the great reason. Barnes and Robertson note the decisive argument: all — both the judge and the judged — must stand before Christ's tribunal; since Christ is the judge of all, we usurp his office when we judge our brother.

The witnesses note the deity implication: the "judgment seat of Christ" (with the better reading "of God" in v.10, confirmed by v.11's citation) ascribes the final judgment to Christ — a mark of his divine dignity. The verse displays the rebuke of judging in view of Christ's judgment seat.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul rebukes both faults — the weak who judges (condemns) and the strong who sets at nought (despises) a brother (Barnes, Robertson) — by the decisive reason that all, both judge and judged, must stand before the judgment seat of Christ; since Christ judges all, we usurp his office in judging our brother. The ascription of the final judgment to Christ marks his divine dignity. The verse displays the rebuke of judging. The verse marks the double fault: judging and despising. The verse displays the reason: the judgment seat. The verse displays the judge: Christ. The verse displays the dignity: Christ's divine office. The rebuke displays the folly of judging whom Christ alone will judge. The verse displays the judging: of the weak. The verse displays the despising: of the strong. The verse displays the tribunal: of Christ. The rebuke of judging, in view of Christ's judgment seat, displays the folly of usurping the office that belongs to Christ alone: since both the judge and the judged must stand before his tribunal, neither the weak nor the strong may condemn or despise a brother. The verse displays the coming universal judgment as the great check on our judging, and confirms it from the prophets (v.11).
11:
"For it is written, As I live, saith the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God." The witnesses note the universal homage foretold — and the deity of Christ. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah (45:23) — God's solemn oath ("as I live") that every knee shall bow and every tongue confess to him; the universal submission of all to God.

On the application to Christ, the witnesses note the force. Since v.10 speaks of the judgment seat (of Christ/God), and this homage in Isaiah is paid to Jehovah, Paul's application of it confirms that all will bow before Christ as judge — a strong testimony to his deity (the words spoken of the LORD applied to Christ).

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the bearing: since every knee shall bow and every tongue give account to God, the judgment belongs to him alone, and our judging of brethren is presumption. The verse displays the universal homage foretold and the dignity of the Judge.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 45:23 — God's solemn oath ("as I live") that every knee shall bow and every tongue confess to him, the universal submission of all to God (Barnes, Robertson); and since this homage to Jehovah is applied in the context of Christ's judgment seat, it confirms that all will bow before Christ as judge — a testimony to his deity. Calvin and Theodoret note the bearing: since all give account to God, the judgment is his alone, and our judging presumption. The verse displays the universal homage. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the oath: as I live. The verse displays the homage: every knee bows. The verse displays the deity: applied to Christ. The universal homage displays the supreme dignity of the Judge before whom all bow. The verse displays the submission: of all. The verse displays the confession: every tongue. The verse displays the Judge: God in Christ. The universal homage foretold — every knee shall bow, every tongue confess to God — displays the supreme dignity of the Judge before whom all must appear: the words spoken of Jehovah, applied here to the judgment of Christ, both confirm his deity and seal the warning against our judging. The verse displays that all will bow and give account to God, and draws the personal conclusion (v.12).
12:
"So then every one of us shall give account of himself to God." The brief verse notes that each shall give account to God. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note the inference from vv.10-11: since all must stand before God's tribunal, every one of us shall give account of himself to God — a personal, individual reckoning.

On "of himself," the witnesses note the personal responsibility. Each gives account of his own conduct, not his brother's — so we should attend to ourselves rather than judging others.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the application: since each must answer for himself, let each look to his own walk and leave his brother to God; the coming reckoning a powerful motive to self-examination, not censoriousness. The verse displays that each shall give account of himself to God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference from vv.10-11 — since all must stand before God's tribunal, every one shall give account of himself to God, a personal individual reckoning (Barnes, Robertson); each answering for his own conduct, not his brother's, so we should attend to ourselves. Calvin and Theodoret note that since each answers for himself, let each look to his own walk and leave his brother to God. The verse displays that each gives account to God. The verse marks the conclusion: so then. The verse displays the reckoning: every one. The verse displays the personal: of himself. The verse displays the application: self, not others. That each shall give account of himself displays the personal responsibility that forbids judging others. The verse displays the account: to God. The verse displays the individuality: each for himself. The verse displays the lesson: mind thyself. That every one shall give account of himself to God displays the personal responsibility that forbids our judging of others: since each must answer to God for his own conduct, he should attend to his own walk rather than condemn his brother. The verse displays the individual reckoning that turns our concern inward, and so forbids putting a stumbling-block before a brother (v.13).
13:
"Let us not therefore judge one another any more: but judge this rather, that no man put a stumblingblock or an occasion to fall in his brother's way." The witnesses note the turn from judging to not offending. On "let us not judge one another any more," the grammatical voices note the resolve. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's conclusion to the first part: let us cease judging (condemning) one another over these matters.

On "judge this rather, that no man put a stumblingblock," the witnesses note the play on "judge." Barnes and Robertson note the wordplay: instead of judging the brother, "judge" (resolve, determine) this — not to put a stumbling-block or occasion of falling in his way; the strong now specially addressed.

On "stumblingblock... occasion to fall," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Theodoret note the duty: the strong must take care that their liberty does not cause the weak to stumble (act against conscience) or fall (into sin or apostasy). The verse displays the turn from judging to not offending a brother.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes the first part — let us cease judging (condemning) one another over these matters (Barnes, Robertson) — and turns by a wordplay: instead of judging the brother, "judge" (resolve) this, not to put a stumbling-block or occasion of falling in his way, the strong now specially addressed; their liberty must not cause the weak to stumble (act against conscience) or fall (Wesley, Theodoret). The verse displays the turn from judging to not offending. The verse marks the resolve: judge not. The verse displays the wordplay: judge this rather. The verse displays the duty: no stumbling-block. The verse displays the care: for the weak. The turn displays the shift from censure to self-restraint for the brother's sake. The verse displays the ceasing: of judging. The verse displays the resolving: not to offend. The verse displays the stumbling-block: to be removed. The turn from judging to not offending a brother — judge this rather, that no man put a stumbling-block in his brother's way — displays the shift from censure to self-restraint: instead of condemning one another, the strong are to resolve not to cause the weak to stumble or fall. The verse displays the new theme of the chapter's second half — the responsibility of the strong toward the weak — and grounds it in the nature of cleanness and uncleanness (v.14).
14:
"I know, and am persuaded by the Lord Jesus, that there is nothing unclean of itself: but to him that esteemeth any thing to be unclean, to him it is unclean." The witnesses note the abolition of ceremonial uncleanness, yet conscience binds. On "nothing unclean of itself," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note Paul, siding with the strong, declares (persuaded "in the Lord Jesus," by the gospel's teaching) that no food is unclean in itself — the ceremonial distinctions abolished, all things pure in their own nature.

On "but to him that esteemeth it unclean, to him it is unclean," the witnesses note the power of conscience. Barnes and Chrysostom note that, though nothing is unclean objectively, to the man who believes it unclean it is so — for him, because he would act against his conscience; uncleanness here is subjective, in the mind of the regarder.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the pastoral point: the weak must not be forced to violate his conscience, for to use what he thinks unclean defiles him; conscience, even when erring, binds until better instructed. The verse displays the abolition of ceremonial uncleanness, yet the binding force of conscience.

The witnesses agree: Paul, siding with the strong, declares (persuaded "in the Lord Jesus," by the gospel) that no food is unclean in itself — the ceremonial distinctions abolished, all pure in their own nature (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin); yet to the man who believes it unclean it is unclean, for he would act against conscience, uncleanness being subjective in the mind of the regarder (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Calvin note the weak must not be forced to violate conscience, which, even when erring, binds until better instructed. The verse displays the abolition of uncleanness yet the binding of conscience. The verse marks the principle: nothing unclean of itself. The verse displays the exception: to him that esteemeth it. The verse displays the subjectivity: in the mind. The verse displays the pastoral care: conscience binds. The verse displays both Christian liberty and the rights of conscience. The verse displays the liberty: all pure. The verse displays the conscience: binding the weak. The verse displays the care: not to force. The abolition of ceremonial uncleanness, yet the binding force of conscience — nothing is unclean of itself, but to him that esteemeth it unclean it is unclean — displays both Christian liberty and the rights of conscience: though the gospel has made all foods pure, the weak brother who thinks a thing unclean would sin by using it against his conscience. The verse displays that liberty must not override a tender conscience, and presses the law of love toward the grieved brother (v.15).
15:
"But if thy brother be grieved with thy meat, now walkest thou not charitably. Destroy not him with thy meat, for whom Christ died." The witnesses note the law of love and the warning not to destroy a brother — the perseverance/atonement crux. On "if thy brother be grieved... walkest not charitably," the grammatical voices note the breach of love. Barnes and Robertson note that to grieve (pain, wound) a brother over food is to violate the law of love — the strong, insisting on his liberty at the cost of the brother's conscience, no longer walks in charity.

On "destroy not him for whom Christ died," the great debate divides. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley take it strictly: a man for whom Christ died may actually perish — the weak brother may be so stumbled as to fall and be lost, proving both that Christ died for such and that a believer may finally fall. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Reformed Calvin take it as the tendency and danger, not the actual event: the language warns of the ruinous direction of such conduct, but the truly elect (for whom Christ died, here = true Christians) will be kept by God, who will not permit their destruction.

The witnesses note the force either way: to wound a soul for whom Christ shed his blood, over a matter of food, is a heinous breach of love. The verse displays the law of love and the warning not to destroy a brother.

The witnesses agree that to grieve a brother over food violates the law of love — the strong, insisting on his liberty at the cost of the brother's conscience, no longer walks charitably (Barnes, Robertson) — and that to "destroy" one for whom Christ died, over meat, is a heinous breach of love. But the verse divides them: Clarke and Wesley take it strictly (a man for whom Christ died may actually perish, proving general atonement and the possibility of final falling); Barnes and Calvin take it as the tendency and danger, not the actual event (the elect being kept by God, who will not permit their ruin). The verse displays the law of love and the warning. The verse marks the breach: not charitably. The verse displays the warning: destroy not. The verse displays the value: for whom Christ died. The verse displays the debate: real perishing or hypothetical danger. The warning displays the preciousness of the brother bought by Christ's blood. The verse displays the grieving: of the brother. The verse displays the love: violated. The verse displays the crux: perseverance and atonement. The law of love and the warning not to destroy a brother for whom Christ died display the gravity of wounding a soul over a trifle: to grieve the weak by one's meat is to walk uncharitably and to endanger one bought by Christ's blood. The verse opens the debate whether this proves a true believer may finally perish (Clarke, Wesley) or warns only of the ruinous tendency, the elect being divinely preserved (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays the supreme worth of the brother as the measure of our self-denial, and guards the good name of the gospel (v.16).
16:
"Let not then your good be evil spoken of:" The brief verse notes guarding the good of Christian liberty from reproach. The grammatical voices note the "good." Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott note "your good" is the blessing of Christian liberty (freedom from the ceremonial law) which the strong rightly enjoy — a real good.

On "be evil spoken of," the witnesses note the danger. Barnes notes the caution: do not so use your liberty as to give occasion for railing, reproach, or blasphemy — lest a good thing (liberty) be spoken against because of the strife and offence its misuse causes.

The eastern Theodoret notes the point: the strong should not let their good (liberty, or the gospel itself) become a cause of injury and reproach; better to forego its use than to bring it into disrepute. The verse displays the guarding of Christian liberty from reproach.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "your good" is the blessing of Christian liberty (freedom from the ceremonial law) which the strong rightly enjoy (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott); and they must not so use it as to give occasion for reproach or blasphemy, lest a good thing be evil spoken of because of the strife its misuse causes (Barnes). Theodoret notes the strong should not let their good become a cause of injury and reproach, better foregoing its use than bringing it into disrepute. The verse displays the guarding of liberty from reproach. The verse marks the good: Christian liberty. The verse displays the danger: evil spoken of. The verse displays the caution: misuse brings reproach. The verse displays the remedy: forego rather than dishonour. The guarding displays the care that liberty not be discredited by its abuse. The verse displays the blessing: liberty. The verse displays the reproach: to be avoided. The verse displays the wisdom: gentle use. The guarding of Christian liberty from reproach — let not your good be evil spoken of — displays the care the strong must take that their real blessing of freedom not be discredited by its uncharitable use: rather than provoke strife and bring liberty into disrepute, they should use it gently or forego it. The verse displays the concern for the gospel's good name, and grounds it in the true nature of the kingdom of God (v.17).
17:
"For the kingdom of God is not meat and drink; but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the great definition of the kingdom of God. On "not meat and drink," the grammatical voices note the negative. Barnes and Robertson note the kingdom of God (the reign of God in the heart, the church, true religion) does not consist in observances about food and drink — externals are not its substance.

On "but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost," the witnesses note the substance — with a difference of emphasis. Barnes, Ellicott, and (here) Calvin take these in the practical sense suited to the context: righteousness (right dealing toward others), peace (concord among brethren), and joy (mutual gladness) wrought by the Holy Spirit. Some (Gill, Clarke) include the deeper graces — justifying righteousness, peace with God, and spiritual joy.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the bearing: since the kingdom consists of these spiritual realities, not of meat and drink, the strong may freely forego their liberty in food without loss — what matters is righteousness, peace, and joy. The verse displays the great definition of the kingdom of God.

The witnesses agree: the kingdom of God (the reign of God in the heart, true religion) does not consist in food and drink, externals not being its substance (Barnes, Robertson) — but in righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. They differ in emphasis: Barnes, Ellicott, and Calvin (here) take these in the practical sense fitting the context (right dealing toward others, concord, mutual gladness wrought by the Spirit), while Gill and Clarke include the deeper graces (justifying righteousness, peace with God, spiritual joy). Calvin and Theodoret note that since the kingdom is these spiritual realities, the strong may forego their food-liberty without loss. The verse displays the great definition. The verse marks the negative: not meat and drink. The verse displays the substance: righteousness, peace, joy. The verse displays the emphasis: practical or deeper graces. The verse displays the source: the Holy Ghost. The definition displays the spiritual nature of God's kingdom. The verse displays the externals: not the substance. The verse displays the graces: the substance. The verse displays the Spirit: their author. The great definition of the kingdom of God — not meat and drink, but righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost — displays its essentially spiritual nature: true religion consists not in observances about food but in right conduct, concord, and Spirit-wrought gladness, whether read of the practical graces among brethren or the deeper blessings of justification, peace with God, and spiritual joy. The verse displays that the substance of the kingdom infinitely outweighs disputes over meat, and commends the service of Christ in these things (v.18).
18:
"For he that in these things serveth Christ is acceptable to God, and approved of men." The witnesses note that serving Christ in these things pleases God and men. On "he that in these things serveth Christ," the grammatical voices note the service. Barnes and Robertson note that he who serves Christ in righteousness, peace, and joy (v.17) — making these, not food disputes, the substance of his religion — truly serves his Master.

On "is acceptable to God," the witnesses note the divine approval. Such a one is well-pleasing to God, who values these spiritual graces and the obedience that produces them.

On "and approved of men," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the human esteem. Barnes notes that such conduct also commends itself to men — even the world acknowledges the excellence of a life of righteousness and peace; Calvin notes this is honest judgment, not the malicious censure of the ungodly. The verse displays that serving Christ in these things pleases God and men.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: he who serves Christ in righteousness, peace, and joy (making these, not food disputes, the substance of his religion) truly serves his Master (Barnes, Robertson) — and is acceptable to God, who values these spiritual graces, and approved of men, his conduct commending itself even to the world (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret and Calvin note this is honest human judgment. The verse displays that serving Christ in these things pleases God and men. The verse marks the service: in these things. The verse displays the approval: acceptable to God. The verse displays the esteem: approved of men. The verse displays the substance: the spiritual graces. Serving Christ in these things displays the double commendation of a spiritual religion. The verse displays the Master: Christ. The verse displays the pleasing: of God. The verse displays the esteem: of men. That serving Christ in these things pleases God and men displays the double commendation of a truly spiritual religion: he who makes righteousness, peace, and joy the substance of his service to Christ is well-pleasing to God and esteemed even by men. The verse displays the worth of a peaceable, righteous walk over contentious disputes, and exhorts to the pursuit of peace and edification (v.19).
19:
"Let us therefore follow after the things which make for peace, and things wherewith one may edify another." The witnesses note the pursuit of peace and edification. On "follow after the things which make for peace," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul exhorts the believers to pursue (follow after, earnestly seek) the things that promote peace — concord and harmony among brethren, rather than strife over indifferent matters.

On "things wherewith one may edify another," the witnesses note the second aim. Barnes and Henry note the goal of edification — building one another up in faith and holiness; conduct is to be measured by whether it promotes a brother's spiritual good.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the rule: peace and mutual edification are the standards by which the use of liberty must be judged; whatever destroys peace or hinders a brother's edifying is to be avoided. The verse displays the pursuit of peace and mutual edification.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul exhorts the believers to pursue (earnestly seek) the things that make for peace — concord among brethren rather than strife over indifferent matters (Barnes, Robertson) — and the things that edify (build up) one another in faith and holiness, conduct being measured by a brother's spiritual good (Barnes, Henry). Calvin and Theodoret note peace and edification are the standards by which the use of liberty must be judged. The verse displays the pursuit of peace and edification. The verse marks the exhortation: follow after peace. The verse displays the aim: edification. The verse displays the standard: a brother's good. The verse displays the avoidance: strife. The pursuit displays peace and edification as the rule of Christian conduct. The verse displays the peace: to be sought. The verse displays the building: of one another. The verse displays the measure: mutual good. The pursuit of peace and mutual edification — follow after the things which make for peace and edify one another — displays the positive rule that should govern the use of liberty: believers are to seek concord and the building up of one another, measuring their conduct by whether it promotes a brother's spiritual good. The verse displays the constructive aim that replaces contention, and warns again against destroying God's work for meat (v.20).
20:
"For meat destroy not the work of God. All things indeed are pure; but it is evil for that man who eateth with offence." The witnesses note the warning not to destroy God's work for food. On "for meat destroy not the work of God," the grammatical voices note the warning. Barnes and Robertson note "the work of God" is either the brother himself (the soul God is renewing) or the work of grace in him; for the sake of food, the strong must not pull down what God is building.

On "all things indeed are pure," the witnesses note the concession. Barnes and Calvin note Paul again affirms the strong man's principle: all foods are indeed pure (clean, lawful) in themselves — the truth conceded.

On "but it is evil for that man who eateth with offence," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the qualification. Though the food is pure, it is evil for the man who eats so as to give offence — causing a brother to stumble; the sin lies not in the food but in the uncharitable use. The verse displays the warning not to destroy God's work for food.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "the work of God" is the brother (or the grace in him) which the strong must not pull down for the sake of food (Barnes, Robertson); and though "all things are pure" (the strong man's principle conceded, Barnes, Calvin), it is evil for the man who eats with offence — causing a brother to stumble, the sin lying not in the food but in the uncharitable use (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the warning not to destroy God's work. The verse marks the warning: destroy not the work of God. The verse displays the concession: all things pure. The verse displays the qualification: evil to eat with offence. The verse displays the locus: the use, not the food. The warning displays the priority of the brother's soul over food. The verse displays the work: of God. The verse displays the purity: of all foods. The verse displays the offence: to be avoided. The warning not to destroy God's work for food — all things are pure, but it is evil to eat with offence — displays the priority of the brother's soul over the indulgence of liberty: though every food is clean, the strong sins if he uses his liberty so as to make a brother stumble. The verse displays that the sin lies in the uncharitable use, not the thing used, and draws the practical conclusion of abstinence (v.21).
21:
"It is good neither to eat flesh, nor to drink wine, nor any thing whereby thy brother stumbleth, or is offended, or is made weak." The witnesses note the rule of loving abstinence for the brother's sake. On "good neither to eat flesh nor drink wine," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lays down the positive rule: it is good (right, noble) to abstain from flesh, wine, or anything else, if its use would cause a brother to stumble — self-denial for love's sake.

On "whereby thy brother stumbleth, or is offended, or is made weak," the witnesses note the threefold harm. Barnes notes the gradation: the brother may stumble (be led to act against conscience), be offended (scandalized), or be made weak (have his faith injured) — any of which the strong should avoid causing.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Theodoret note the breadth: the principle extends beyond food to anything (any indifferent thing) that would harm a brother; love willingly forgoes its rights. The verse displays the rule of loving abstinence for the brother's sake.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul lays down the positive rule — it is good (right, noble) to abstain from flesh, wine, or anything else if its use would cause a brother to stumble, be offended, or be made weak (Barnes, Robertson) — self-denial for love's sake, the gradation marking the threefold harm to be avoided. Wesley and Theodoret note the principle extends to anything indifferent that would harm a brother, love willingly forgoing its rights. The verse displays the rule of loving abstinence. The verse marks the rule: good to abstain. The verse displays the scope: flesh, wine, anything. The verse displays the harm: stumble, offend, weaken. The verse displays the motive: love. The rule displays self-denial for the brother's sake. The verse displays the abstinence: willing. The verse displays the brother: to be spared. The verse displays the love: forgoing rights. The rule of loving abstinence for the brother's sake — good to abstain from flesh, wine, or anything whereby a brother stumbles — displays the self-denial that love requires: the strong should willingly forgo even lawful things rather than cause a brother to stumble, be scandalized, or have his faith weakened. The verse displays the noble principle of surrendering one's liberty for love, and counsels how to hold one's faith (v.22).
22:
"Hast thou faith? have it to thyself before God. Happy is he that condemneth not himself in that thing which he alloweth." The witnesses note that faith is to be held before God, with a self-approving conscience. On "hast thou faith? have it to thyself before God," the grammatical voices note the counsel. Barnes and Robertson note "faith" here means the strong man's persuasion that all things are lawful; he is to hold this conviction privately, before God — not to parade it ostentatiously where it would give offence.

On "happy is he that condemneth not himself in that which he alloweth," the witnesses note the beatitude. Barnes and Calvin note the blessedness of a clear conscience: happy is the man who does not condemn himself in what he approves and does — who acts with a conscience untroubled by self-reproach.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Theodoret note the application: the strong should enjoy his liberty before God without flaunting it, and is blessed if his conscience approves his conduct; better to forgo a thing than to act with self-condemnation. The verse displays that faith is to be held before God, with a self-approving conscience.

The witnesses agree: "faith" here is the strong man's persuasion that all things are lawful, to be held privately before God, not paraded ostentatiously where it would offend (Barnes, Robertson); and "happy is he that condemneth not himself in that which he alloweth" — the blessedness of a clear conscience, the man who does not condemn himself in what he approves and does (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley and Theodoret note the strong should enjoy his liberty before God without flaunting it, blessed if his conscience approves. The verse displays faith held before God with a clear conscience. The verse marks the counsel: have faith to thyself. The verse displays the privacy: before God. The verse displays the beatitude: condemns not himself. The verse displays the blessing: a clear conscience. That faith is held before God displays the wisdom of a private, self-approving conviction. The verse displays the faith: the strong man's persuasion. The verse displays the reserve: before God. The verse displays the happiness: an untroubled conscience. That faith is to be held before God, with a self-approving conscience — happy is he that condemneth not himself in that which he alloweth — displays the wisdom of a private, settled conviction: the strong should enjoy his liberty before God without flaunting it, and is blessed if his conscience does not reproach him in what he does. The verse displays the rare felicity of a conscience untroubled by self-condemnation, and warns the doubter against acting against his (v.23).
23:
"And he that doubteth is damned if he eat, because he eateth not of faith: for whatsoever is not of faith is sin." The witnesses note the condemnation of acting against a doubting conscience — and the great maxim. On "he that doubteth is damned if he eat," the grammatical voices note the sentence. Barnes and Robertson note that the doubter (one not persuaded a thing is lawful) is "damned" (condemned — not necessarily hell, but adjudged guilty) if he eats, because he acts against his conscience, eating "not of faith" (without the conviction that it is right).

On "for whatsoever is not of faith is sin," the great maxim divides interpreters in its scope. Barnes and Ellicott take it as a general principle covering this particular case: whatever is done without the conviction that it is right (against or without a persuaded conscience) is sin — but the verse does not (against Augustine) prove that every act of an unbeliever is sin. Wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the broader Augustinian reading (taken up in the Church's Article XIII) that all works done without saving faith have the nature of sin, while granting the immediate sense is the doubting conscience.

The witnesses note the practical rule: one must never act against conscience; what is done in doubt, without the persuasion of its lawfulness, is sin. The verse displays the condemnation of acting against a doubting conscience and the maxim that whatever is not of faith is sin.

The witnesses agree: the doubter is "condemned" if he eats, because he acts against his conscience, eating "not of faith" (without the conviction that it is right, Barnes, Robertson); and "whatsoever is not of faith is sin" — whatever is done without the persuasion that it is right is sin. Its scope divides them: Barnes and Ellicott take it as a general principle covering this case (against Augustine, not proving every unbeliever's act is sin), while Clarke notes the broader Augustinian reading (Article XIII — all works without saving faith have the nature of sin), granting the immediate sense is the doubting conscience. The verse displays the condemnation of acting against conscience. The verse marks the doubter: condemned if he eat. The verse displays the cause: not of faith. The verse displays the maxim: whatever is not of faith is sin. The verse displays the debate: contextual or Augustinian. The maxim displays the binding force of a persuaded conscience. The verse displays the doubt: condemning. The verse displays the faith: the conviction of right. The verse displays the principle: not of faith, sin. The condemnation of acting against a doubting conscience, and the maxim that whatsoever is not of faith is sin, display the binding force of a persuaded conscience: the doubter who eats against his conviction sins, because he acts without faith — read narrowly of the doubting conscience (Barnes, Ellicott) or broadly of all works without saving faith (Clarke, after Augustine). The verse displays the great principle that conscience must never be violated, and closes the chapter's counsel on the weak and the strong.
